title: Modern campaigns
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on how campaign organizations and strategies affect the electoral process

assessment_items.question_content:
![The bar graph shows the length of national election cycles in the United States in 2016, Mexico, the United Kingdom in 2015, Canada in 2015, France, and Japan.  The X-axis indicates number of days and the Y-axis indicates the country. The countries are listed in descending order from longest to shortest with the United States having the longest election cycle that lasted 281 days. Mexico's lasted 147 days, the UK's lasted 139 days, and Canada's lasted 78 days. France and Japan had the shortest elections that lasted 14 days and 12 days respectively. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a0fd8e9a15d35eba7f608a903f94543b.svg)

>Data from: [NPR](https://www.npr.org/sections/itsallpolitics/2015/10/21/450238156/canadas-11-week-campaign-reminds-us-that-american-elections-are-much-longer)

**Which of the following statements is reflected in the data in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States election cycle lasts significantly longer than other countries around the world.
Election cycles in the United States have gotten longer over the past fifty years.
The US government is placing limits on election cycles to match the cadence of other major countries.
Election cycles tend to fluctuate as major events speed up or slow down the process.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The bar graph shows the length of national election cycles in the United States in 2016, Mexico, the United Kingdom in 2015, Canada in 2015, France, and Japan.  The X-axis indicates number of days and the Y-axis indicates the country. The countries are listed in descending order from longest to shortest with the United States having the longest election cycle that lasted 281 days. Mexico's lasted 147 days, the UK's lasted 139 days, and Canada's lasted 78 days. France and Japan had the shortest elections that lasted 14 days and 12 days respectively.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a0fd8e9a15d35eba7f608a903f94543b.svg)

>Data from: [NPR](https://www.npr.org/sections/itsallpolitics/2015/10/21/450238156/canadas-11-week-campaign-reminds-us-that-american-elections-are-much-longer)

**Which of the following is a potential consequence of the trend illustrated in the bar chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: an increase in campaign costs as candidates tend to run for at least a year before the election
an over-reliance on political parties as they have to campaign for candidates up for reelection
an increase in voter turnout as people can get to know candidates better as they run for office
a shift towards grassroots fundraising and campaigning as candidates can spend time in small communities

assessment_items.question_content:
![The bar graph shows the length of national election cycles in the United States in 2016, Mexico, the United Kingdom in 2015, Canada in 2015, France, and Japan.  The X-axis indicates number of days and the Y-axis indicates the country. The countries are listed in descending order from longest to shortest with the United States having the longest election cycle that lasted 281 days. Mexico's lasted 147 days, the UK's lasted 139 days, and Canada's lasted 78 days. France and Japan had the shortest elections that lasted 14 days and 12 days respectively.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a0fd8e9a15d35eba7f608a903f94543b.svg)

>Data from: [NPR](https://www.npr.org/sections/itsallpolitics/2015/10/21/450238156/canadas-11-week-campaign-reminds-us-that-american-elections-are-much-longer)

**Based on the information in the bar chart, which of the following is the most likely implication of long election cycles in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Candidates running for reelection spend a significant portion of their terms campaigning for reelection.
Incumbents no longer have an advantage in name recognition in elections, as voters can get to know all candidates.
Voter turnout has increased as people know well in advance when an election is happening in their communities.
Terms in the House of Representatives have increased to allow people seeking reelection enough time to campaign.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The two graphs show the average cost of winning elections in the House of Representatives and the Senate since 1986. The cost accounts for inflation by showing the cost in 2018 dollars. The House of Representatives saw a 150 percent increase from 1986 to 2018, steadily increasing from 800 thousand dollars in 1986 to 2 million in 2018. The Senate saw a 112 percent increase from 1986 to 2018, increasing from 7 million dollars in 1986 to nearly 15 million in 2018. However, the cost did not steadily increasing, dipping significantly in 2002. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/28730a742bd1769a9139c53569b7650a.svg)

**Which of the following statements reflects a pattern in the line graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The cost of congressional campaigns has risen over the past thirty years.
The cost of House campaigns has remained relatively the same, but costs have increased for Senate campaigns.
The cost of congressional campaigns has declined over the past twenty years.
The cost of congressional campaigns decreases during midterm election years.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The two graphs show the average cost of winning elections in the House of Representatives and the Senate since 1986. The cost accounts for inflation by showing the cost in 2018 dollars. The House of Representatives saw a 150 percent increase from 1986 to 2018, steadily increasing from 800 thousand dollars in 1986 to 2 million in 2018. The Senate saw a 112 percent increase from 1986 to 2018, increasing from 7 million dollars in 1986 to nearly 15 million in 2018. However, the cost did not steadily increasing, dipping significantly in 2002.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/28730a742bd1769a9139c53569b7650a.svg)

**Which of the following is a likely effect of the trend depicted in the line graph on elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Members of Congress have to spend a significant portion of their time fundraising to win reelection.
Average Americans have little influence on their congressional representatives because they cannot financially support their campaigns.
Members of Congress have looked towards financial support from international donors.
Interest groups and PACs have lost their influence on members of Congress as they’re focused on fundraising efforts.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The two graphs show the average cost of winning elections in the House of Representatives and the Senate since 1986. The cost accounts for inflation by showing the cost in 2018 dollars. The House of Representatives saw a 150 percent increase from 1986 to 2018, steadily increasing from 800 thousand dollars in 1986 to 2 million in 2018. The Senate saw a 112 percent increase from 1986 to 2018, increasing from 7 million dollars in 1986 to nearly 15 million in 2018. However, the cost did not steadily increasing, dipping significantly in 2002.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/28730a742bd1769a9139c53569b7650a.svg)

**The data in the graph best illustrates which of the following common criticisms of modern elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The rising costs of political campaigns make it harder for unknown challengers to beat incumbents who have had years to fundraise for their reelection campaigns.
The influence of social media has made it easier for people seeking political office to raise money, leading to a rise in the costs of campaigns.
The role of political consultants in campaigns has led to candidates for office raising money for their help to win a seat in Congress.
The election cycle has become significantly shorter as fundraising efforts have declined because of caps set by Congress.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The line graph shows the number of articles in major newspapers that mentioned political consultants from 1970 to 2004. In 1970, only one article mentioned political consultants and that stayed below 100 until 1983 when it jumped to 110. The line spikes and dips from 1984 to 2004. We see major spikes in 1986 to 606 mentions, in 1988 to 853 mentions, and 1992 to 998 mentions. In 2004, there were 679 articles that included mentions of political consultants.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e8cba84de912d3d62b2bf9378c1372c2.svg)

**Which of the following statements accurately describes the information presented in the line graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Newspapers started mentioning the use of political consultants in 1970, and it has continued to increase.
Candidates for political office have relied heavily on political consultants to organize their campaigns.
New opportunities opened up for political consultants as the role of the presidency expanded.
Media reports on elections as if they are horse races, leading to more of a spotlight on political news.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The line graph shows the number of articles in major newspapers that mentioned political consultants from 1970 to 2004. In 1970, only one article mentioned political consultants and that stayed below 100 until 1983 when it jumped to 110. The line spikes and dips from 1984 to 2004. We see major spikes in 1986 to 606 mentions, in 1988 to 853 mentions, and 1992 to 998 mentions. In 2004, there were 679 articles that included mentions of political consultants.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e8cba84de912d3d62b2bf9378c1372c2.svg)

**Which of the following is a potential consequence of the trend illustrated in the line graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the professionalization of elections as most candidates have relied on political consultants
the rise of party-centered campaigns as consultants are usually supplied by political parties
the emergence of grassroots campaigns in which supporters take to social media to campaign for their candidate
the decline in media attention for political campaigns as elections have become less competitive

assessment_items.question_content:
![The line graph shows the number of articles in major newspapers that mentioned political consultants from 1970 to 2004. In 1970, only one article mentioned political consultants and that stayed below 100 until 1983 when it jumped to 110. The line spikes and dips from 1984 to 2004. We see major spikes in 1986 to 606 mentions, in 1988 to 853 mentions, and 1992 to 998 mentions. In 2004, there were 679 articles that included mentions of political consultants.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e8cba84de912d3d62b2bf9378c1372c2.svg)

**Based on the information in the line graph, which of the following is the most likely implication of the increasing dependence on political consultants?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a change in how candidates work with political parties and interact with voters
a decline in campaign spending as consultants have the tools to curtail unnecessary spending
a new strategy in which candidates try to separate themselves from the party’s policy agenda
a shift away from relying on polling data to determine campaign messaging towards relying on social media trends

assessment_items.question_content:
![The infographic compares social media usage for ten presidential candidates still in contention as of February 7, 2020. The ten candidates are Trump, Biden, Bloomberg, Buttigieg, Gabbard, Klobaucher, Sanders, Steyer, Warren, and Yang. All candidates post on a daily basis on Facebook and Twitter. Half of the candidates (Trump, Biden, Buttigieg, Gabbard, and Klobaucher) post on Instagram on a weekly basis while the remaining five candidates post on a daily basis. Three candidates (Biden, Bloomberg, and Sanders) post on YouTube on a weekly basis, and the remaining seven candidates post on a less than weekly basis. Only four candidates have Twitch accounts, and three of them (Trump, Biden, and Sanders) post on a daily basis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71c75d3983b8e33e0fb09c28199b5f92.svg)

**Based on the information in the graphic, which of the following claims would an opponent of the use of social media most likely make?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Social media and how candidates use it takes attention away from policy issues and towards political scandals.
Social media allows candidates to connect directly with voters, giving them a better idea of the needs of the electorate.
Social media has not affected elections, so campaigns should stop wasting money on social media advertisements.
Social media profiles have increased the professionalization of campaigns, signaling that candidates are too elite for the average voter.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The infographic compares social media usage for ten presidential candidates still in contention as of February 7, 2020. The ten candidates are Trump, Biden, Bloomberg, Buttigieg, Gabbard, Klobaucher, Sanders, Steyer, Warren, and Yang. All candidates post on a daily basis on Facebook and Twitter. Half of the candidates (Trump, Biden, Buttigieg, Gabbard, and Klobaucher) post on Instagram on a weekly basis while the remaining five candidates post on a daily basis. Three candidates (Biden, Bloomberg, and Sanders) post on YouTube on a weekly basis, and the remaining seven candidates post on a less than weekly basis. Only four candidates have Twitch accounts, and three of them (Trump, Biden, and Sanders) post on a daily basis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71c75d3983b8e33e0fb09c28199b5f92.svg)

**Based on your knowledge and the infographic, which of the following is true regarding the role of social media in modern elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Candidates have used social media as an avenue to tailor their political messages and reach out to voters directly.
Candidates use social media to raise funds from these social media organizations for their campaigns.
Candidates do not use social media consistently enough for it to impact elections.
Candidates do not directly interact with voters, instead relying on their campaigns to handle social media profiles.

assessment_items.question_content:
![The infographic compares social media usage for ten presidential candidates still in contention as of February 7, 2020. The ten candidates are Trump, Biden, Bloomberg, Buttigieg, Gabbard, Klobaucher, Sanders, Steyer, Warren, and Yang. All candidates post on a daily basis on Facebook and Twitter. Half of the candidates (Trump, Biden, Buttigieg, Gabbard, and Klobaucher) post on Instagram on a weekly basis while the remaining five candidates post on a daily basis. Three candidates (Biden, Bloomberg, and Sanders) post on YouTube on a weekly basis, and the remaining seven candidates post on a less than weekly basis. Only four candidates have Twitch accounts, and three of them (Trump, Biden, and Sanders) post on a daily basis.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71c75d3983b8e33e0fb09c28199b5f92.svg)

**Which of the following statements best describes the information in the infographic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Most candidates use social media at high rates to engage with voters directly.
Most candidates have personal social media profiles, but do not use it for campaigning.
Democratic candidates are more likely than Republican candidates to use social media.
Republican candidates are more likely to use social media to engage with voters over any other avenue.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is a key difference between election cycles in the United States and those in many other democracies? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: U.S. elections are longer and require nearly constant fundraising.
U.S. elections are shorter and involve less media coverage.
U.S. elections are held more frequently and involve fewer campaign rules.
Other democracies prohibit all political advertising to reduce campaign costs.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Supreme Court's decision in *Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission* (2010) most directly caused which development in campaign strategy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Super PACs began using unlimited funds on independent digital advertisements.
Political parties were granted oversight over all campaign advertisements.
Candidates became subject to stricter spending limits on internet-based advertisements.
Congress prohibited corporations from creating election-related advertisements.

assessment_items.question_content:
A U.S. Senate candidate hires a media consultant to design television and social media ads, test different campaign messages through polling and focus groups, and quickly respond to negative attacks from opponents. The candidate’s campaign team also includes specialists in data analytics, fundraising, and digital outreach.

**Which trend in modern campaigning does this situation best illustrate?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Campaigns have become increasingly professionalized and rely on specialized expertise.
Political parties increasingly guide candidates through campaign messaging and media strategy.
Federal election laws require candidates to work with approved campaign professionals.
Interest groups regularly manage campaign operations for major-party candidates.
